name: Hare Allocator API
summary: Hare is C-like: the runtime exposes `malloc`, `realloc`, `free_`, and `setheap` (swap the internal heap, with caller responsibility for matching `free`/`delete`). Freestanding programs supply their own allocation hooks for dynamic slice operations.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Hare takes the C-like position: the runtime exposes `malloc`, `realloc`, `free_`, and `setheap` (which swaps the internal heap, with caller responsibility for matching `free`/`delete`). Freestanding programs can supply their own `malloc`/`free`/`ensure`/`unensure` so the compiler's dynamic slice operations work without the full runtime.

Hare makes the heap replaceable at a small ABI boundary but does not attempt allocator plumbing through library APIs. This is the minimalist option in the chapter 7.12 family: less ergonomic than Odin or Beef, but with a smaller surface area for the runtime/allocator contract.

sections.title: Freestanding hooks and setheap
sections.content:
`setheap` swaps the runtime's internal heap implementation at runtime; allocations made before the swap must still be freed by the previous heap. Freestanding mode (no host libc) requires the program to provide `malloc`, `free`, `ensure`, and `unensure` so the compiler-generated code for dynamic slice growth and shrinkage links cleanly.

The API surface is small enough to retarget for embedded contexts (RTOSes, bare-metal microcontrollers) without rewriting library code. The trade-off is that there is no per-call or per-data-structure allocator override — the heap is global and replaceable but not parameterised.
